1.title: The letter to the Corinthians
1.text: Paul founded the church at Corinth and wrote this letter to correct divisions and problems that had been reported to him. The city was a great Greek port, rich and cosmopolitan, but also known for its immorality.

1.question: To whom does Paul address this letter?
options: To the church of God which is at Corinth

1.title: The factions of Corinth
1.text: The church was split into groups claiming to be of Paul, of Apollos, of Cephas (Peter), or of Christ. Paul indignantly asks, 'Is Christ divided?', showing that the unity of believers is in Christ, not in human leaders.

1.template: I am of Paul; and I of {0}; and I of Cephas;

1.question: For what did Christ send Paul, according to himself?
options: To preach the gospel, not to baptize

1.template: the preaching of the cross is to them that perish {0};

1.title: Christ crucified
1.text: Paul sums up his message: 'we preach Christ crucified'. To the Jews, who asked for signs, this was a stumblingblock; to the Greeks, who sought wisdom, it was foolishness. But to the called, it is the power and the wisdom of God.

1.question: What did God choose to confound the wise and the mighty?
options: The foolish and weak things of this world

pairs.right: Prosperous Greek port where Paul founded the church
Groups claiming to be of Paul, Apollos, Cephas or Christ
Foolishness to those who perish, power of God to the saved
A stumblingblock to Jews, foolishness to Greeks

2.title: Christ, and him crucified
2.text: Before a city that admired eloquence, Paul determined to know 'nothing, save Jesus Christ, and him crucified'. His strength was not in persuasive words, but in the demonstration of the Spirit and of power.

2.question: Why did the princes of this world not know the wisdom of God?
options: Had they known it, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory
Because it was written in the law

2.title: What eye hath not seen
2.text: To speak of the things God has prepared for those who love him, Paul cites a passage drawn from Isaiah: 'Eye hath not seen, nor ear heard, neither have entered into the heart of man.' Such things are known only by the Spirit.

2.question: Why does the natural man not understand the things of the Spirit of God?
options: Because they are hidden in the law

2.template: But we have the {0} of Christ.

3.question: What sign of immaturity does Paul point out in the Corinthians?
options: Envying, strife and divisions, saying 'I am of Paul'

3.title: I planted, Apollos watered
3.text: Paul uses farming to explain the role of leaders: he planted, Apollos watered, but it was God who gave the increase. Neither the one who plants nor the one who waters is anything, but only God, who makes it grow.

3.template: Apollos watered; but God gave the {0}.
other {0} can no man lay than that is laid,

3.title: Work tested by fire
3.text: Paul says each one's work will be revealed by fire on the day of judgment: what is gold, silver and precious stones will endure; what is wood, hay and stubble will be burned. The person may be saved, but only as through fire.

3.template: Know ye not that ye are the {0} of God,

3.question: What does Paul say about the wisdom of this world?
options: It is the way to God

pairs.right: Servants: one planted, another watered
The one who gives the increase
Jesus Christ, the only possible one
The temple of God, in which the Spirit dwells

4.title: Stewards of the mysteries
4.text: Paul asks that leaders be seen as ministers of Christ and stewards of the mysteries of God. Of a steward one thing above all is required: that he be faithful.

4.template: it is required in stewards, that a man be found {0}.
and what hast thou that thou didst not {0}?

4.title: Fools for Christ
4.text: With irony, Paul contrasts the Corinthians, who thought themselves wise, rich and honored, with the apostles, 'fools for Christ's sake', weak and despised. They suffered hunger, thirst and persecution, and were treated as the filth of the world.

4.question: How does Paul describe the life of the apostles?
options: They suffer hunger, thirst and persecution, and are as the filth of the world
They live in wealth and honor

4.question: How does Paul describe himself in relation to the Corinthians?
options: As a father who begot them in Christ through the gospel
As a king of the saints

4.template: the kingdom of God is not in word, but in {0}.

pairs.right: Leaders of whom faithfulness is required
All was received, nothing is one's own merit
Fools for Christ, a spectacle to the world
Is not in word, but in power

5.question: What action does Paul determine regarding the one who committed grave sin in the church?
options: To deliver him to Satan, that the spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord

5.title: Christ, our passover
5.text: The image of leaven comes from the Jewish Passover, in which every house removed the old leaven before the feast. Paul applies it to the church: 'Christ, our passover, is sacrificed for us', and so they must live with sincerity and truth.

5.template: a little {0} leaveneth the whole lump?

5.question: What does Paul command be done with the one who persists in grave sin in the church?
options: Put him away from among them

6.title: The saints shall judge the world
6.text: To rebuke the brethren who sued one another before pagan judges, Paul reminds them that the saints shall judge the world, and even angels. If they will judge such great things, how could they not settle small matters among themselves?

6.question: What privilege does Paul recall to the saints to rebuke their lawsuits?
options: That the saints shall judge the world

6.question: What fault does Paul point out in brethren taking each other to law?
options: It is already a fault; it would be better to suffer the wrong
There is no problem at all

6.template: but ye are {0}, but ye are sanctified,

6.question: For whom is the body, according to Paul?
options: For the Lord, and the Lord for the body

6.title: Bought with a price
6.text: Paul teaches that the believer's body is the temple of the Holy Ghost, and that they are not their own: they were 'bought with a price'. Therefore they must glorify God both in body and in spirit.

6.template: your body is the {0} of the Holy Ghost which is in you,

7.title: Answers to the Corinthians' questions
7.text: From here on, Paul answers questions the Corinthians themselves had written to him, beginning with marriage. His guidance reflects the conviction that 'the time is short' and the end near.

7.question: Why does Paul say each should have his own spouse?
What does Paul advise those who cannot contain themselves?
options: That they marry, for it is better to marry than to burn

7.question: What command does Paul attribute to the Lord about marriage?
options: That the wife should not depart from her husband

7.template: And so ordain I in all {0}.

7.title: Circumcision is nothing
7.text: Paul repeats a theme dear to him: before God, being circumcised or not matters nothing; what counts is the keeping of the commandments of God. Each should remain in the condition in which he was called.

7.template: but the keeping of the {0} of God.

7.question: How does Paul describe the servant who is called by the Lord?
options: He is the Lord's freeman; and the free is Christ's servant
He must flee from his master
He must buy his freedom first

7.title: The time is short
7.text: Paul guides believers to live as those who keep their eyes on the end: that the married be as though they were not, and those who use the world, as not abusing it, for 'the fashion of this world passeth away'.

7.template: for the fashion of this {0} passeth away.

7.question: With what is the unmarried concerned, according to Paul?
options: With the things of the Lord, how to please the Lord

7.question: For how long is the married woman bound to her husband, according to Paul?
options: As long as her husband lives

pairs.right: Let each have a spouse, to avoid immorality
To marry or stay single: each has his own
Is nothing; what counts is keeping the commandments
Living with eyes on the end, for the world passes

8.title: Meat offered to idols
8.text: In Corinth, much of the meat in the market came from sacrifices made in pagan temples. The Christians debated whether they could eat it. Paul opens the subject with a principle: 'knowledge puffeth up, but charity edifieth'.

8.question: How many gods and lords does Paul affirm there are for Christians?
options: One God, the Father, and one Lord, Jesus Christ

8.question: Why does Paul limit the use of the freedom to eat what was offered to idols?
options: So as not to make the weak brother, for whom Christ died, perish

8.template: I will eat no {0} while the world standeth,

9.title: The apostle's right
9.text: Paul defends his apostleship and his rights, among them being supported by the church, like the other apostles. Even with that right, he chose to preach free of charge, so as to put no obstacle in the way of the gospel.

9.question: What does Paul ask to defend his apostleship?
options: Am I not an apostle? Have I not seen Jesus Christ our Lord?

9.template: Thou shalt not muzzle the mouth of the {0} that treadeth out the corn.

9.question: How does Paul feel about preaching the gospel?
options: It is a duty laid upon him: 'woe is me, if I preach not the gospel!'
He does it for pleasure and fame

9.title: All things to all men
9.text: To win the most, Paul adapted to each group: as a Jew to the Jews, as weak to the weak. 'I am made all things to all men, that I might by all means save some.'

9.question: What strategy does Paul describe in his ministry?
options: He became all things to all men, to by all means save some
He preached only to the strong

9.title: The race in the stadium
9.text: Paul compares the Christian life to a race: all run, but only one receives the prize. The athlete disciplines himself for a corruptible crown; the Christian, for an incorruptible one. Corinth hosted the famous Isthmian Games.

pairs.right: Apostle who gave up his rights for the gospel
Image of the law that the worker deserves support
Adapting to each one to save some
Image of the discipline for an incorruptible crown

10.title: The Rock was Christ
10.text: Paul rereads the exodus: the cloud and the sea were like a baptism, and the water that flowed from the rock was spiritual drink. He declares that 'that Rock was Christ', present with Israel already in the wilderness.

10.question: Why were these stories of Israel written, according to Paul?
options: As examples and a warning for us

10.template: but God is {0}, who will not suffer you to be tempted above that ye are able;

10.question: What do the cup of blessing and the bread we break represent, according to Paul?
options: The communion of the blood and body of Christ

10.question: What does Paul say is impossible to do at the same time?
options: Drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of devils

10.question: What principle does Paul give about lawful things?
options: All are lawful, but not all are expedient nor edify

10.title: All to the glory of God
10.text: Paul sums up the matter of freedom in a broad principle: 'whether ye eat, or drink, or whatsoever ye do, do all to the glory of God', without being a stumbling block to anyone.

10.template: do all to the {0} of God.

11.title: The custom of the veil
11.text: Paul addresses a custom of Corinthian culture about covering or not covering the head when praying and prophesying. More important than the custom is the principle he states: in the Lord, man and woman depend on each other, and all things come from God.

11.question: Whom does Paul ask the believers to follow?
options: Himself, even as he follows Christ

11.question: What does Paul affirm about man and woman 'in the Lord'?
options: Neither is the man without the woman, nor the woman without the man
The man does not need the woman

11.question: What does Paul say about a woman's long hair?
options: It is a glory to her, for her hair is given for a covering

11.question: What does Paul criticize in the Corinthians' gatherings?
options: Each eats his own supper, with divisions; it is not the Lord's supper

11.title: The Lord's Supper
11.text: Verses 23 to 26 contain the earliest account of the institution of the Lord's Supper — written before the Gospels. Paul says he received it from the Lord himself: the bread is the body, and the cup, the new testament in Christ's blood.

11.template: this is my {0}, which is broken for you:

11.question: What does Paul command each to do before eating the bread and drinking the cup?
options: That he confess to the priest

11.question: What does Paul command when the believers come together to eat?
options: To sit by order of age

pairs.right: Cultural custom about covering the head when praying
Instituted by Jesus: the bread and the cup in remembrance of him
What each must do before partaking
How they should act when gathering to eat

12.title: Diversity of gifts
12.text: Paul teaches that there are many gifts — wisdom, knowledge, faith, healing, miracles, prophecy, tongues — but one Spirit who distributes them to each as he will. The variety serves the common good, not individual vanity.

12.template: no man can say that Jesus is the {0}, but by the Holy Ghost.

12.question: Who distributes the various spiritual gifts, according to Paul?
options: One and the same Spirit, as he will

12.title: One body, many members
12.text: Paul compares the church to a body: many members, but one body. The foot is no less of the body for not being a hand, and the eye cannot do without the hand. Each part is necessary, and the weaker members receive greater honor.

12.template: For as the {0} is one, and hath many members,

12.question: What can the eye not say to the hand, according to Paul?
options: I have no need of thee

12.question: What happens when one member of the body suffers, according to Paul?
options: All the members suffer with it

12.question: Which offices did God set first in the church, according to Paul?
options: First apostles, then prophets, then teachers

12.title: A more excellent way
12.text: After dealing with the gifts, Paul points to something greater: 'I shew unto you a more excellent way'. That way is love, the theme of chapter 13, without which no gift has value.

pairs.right: Diverse, distributed by one Spirit
Image of the church: many members, one body
Receive greater honor
Love, of the next chapter

13.title: The hymn to love
13.text: This is one of the best-known texts in the Bible. Paul shows that without love — charity, from the Greek agape — nothing is worth anything: not tongues, nor prophecy, nor giving all to the poor. Love is the more excellent way he had promised to show.

13.template: and have not {0}, I am become as sounding brass,

13.question: How does Paul describe charity?
options: Patient, kind, not envious; bears all and endures all things

13.title: Through a glass, darkly
13.text: Paul says that now we see 'through a glass, darkly', but then face to face. Ancient mirrors, of polished metal, gave imperfect images — a figure of our partial knowledge in this life, which one day will be complete.

13.question: Which three remain, according to Paul, the greatest being charity?

13.template: but the greatest of these is {0}.

14.title: Tongues and prophecy
14.text: In Corinth, the gift of speaking in tongues was highly prized. Paul teaches that prophesying is more useful, because it edifies the whole church, while one who speaks in an unknown tongue edifies only himself, unless there is interpretation.

14.question: What is the difference between one who speaks in tongues and one who prophesies, according to Paul?
options: He who speaks in tongues edifies himself; he who prophesies edifies the church

14.template: if the {0} give an uncertain sound, who shall prepare himself to the battle?

14.question: What does Paul advise the one who speaks in an unknown tongue?
options: That he pray that he may interpret
That he teach others the tongue

14.template: and I will pray with the {0} also:

14.question: What does Paul prefer in the church, rather than ten thousand words in an unknown tongue?
options: Five words with his understanding, to teach others

14.title: All for edification
14.text: Paul regulates worship: let everything — psalm, doctrine, tongue, revelation, interpretation — be done for edification. Tongues must be interpreted, and the prophets speak in turn, for God is a God of peace, not of confusion.

14.question: What principle should govern all that is done when the church gathers?
options: That all things be done for edification

14.question: How does Paul regulate speaking in tongues in the meeting?
options: By two or three, in turn, and let one interpret

14.template: God is not the author of confusion, but of {0},
Let all things be done decently and in {0}.

pairs.right: Edifies the whole church
Edifies oneself, unless interpreted
Image of unintelligible words
Is a God of peace, not of confusion

15.title: The earliest creed
15.text: Verses 3 to 5 contain a formula Paul says he received and passed on: Christ died for our sins, was buried and rose again on the third day, according to the scriptures. It is one of the earliest Christian confessions of faith.

15.template: and that he {0} again the third day according to the scriptures:

15.question: How does Paul describe himself among the apostles?
options: The least of the apostles, unworthy for having persecuted the church

15.title: They denied the resurrection
15.text: Some Corinthians, influenced by Greek philosophy, which despised the body, denied the resurrection of the dead. Paul answers that without it, all faith would be vain, for then Christ also would not have risen.

15.template: then is our preaching {0}, and your faith is also vain.
For as in {0} all die, even so in Christ shall all be made alive.
The last enemy that shall be destroyed is {0}.

15.question: What saying does Paul quote to warn about bad company?
With what image does Paul explain the resurrection of the body?
options: The seed that is only quickened if it first dies

15.title: Different glories
15.text: To speak of the resurrected bodies, Paul points to the sky: the sun, the moon and the stars have different glories, and one star differs from another in glory. So also will be the resurrection of the dead, in transformed bodies.

15.question: How does Paul contrast the first and the last Adam?
options: The first Adam was made a living soul; the last, a quickening spirit
The last was made a living soul

15.template: at the last {0}: for the trumpet shall sound,

15.title: Victory over death
15.text: Paul proclaims the triumph of the resurrection with words drawn from Hosea: 'O death, where is thy sting?' The sting of death is sin, but God gives the victory through Jesus Christ.

15.question: How does Paul conclude the chapter on the resurrection?
options: Be steadfast, abounding in the work of the Lord, for your labour is not in vain

pairs.right: Rose and is the firstfruits of those who sleep
Through him death came to all men
Death, which will be destroyed
Sown natural, raised spiritual

16.title: The collection for the saints
16.text: Paul organizes a collection for the poor church of Jerusalem, asking that each one, on the first day of the week, lay something aside as he prospers. This project united Jewish and Gentile Christians in one fellowship.

16.question: What instruction does Paul give about the collection for the saints?
options: On the first day of the week, let each lay aside as he prospers
That they sell all their goods
That they wait for him to arrive

16.question: Where does Paul say he will stay until Pentecost?

16.title: Stand fast in the faith
16.text: Near the end of the letter, Paul sums it all up in a vigorous exhortation: 'Watch ye, stand fast in the faith, quit you like men, be strong.' And he adds the principle that governs the whole letter: 'Let all your things be done with charity.'

16.template: Watch ye, stand fast in the {0}, quit you like men, be strong.

16.question: How does Paul describe the household of Stephanas?
options: The firstfruits of Achaia, devoted to the ministry of the saints
The founders of the church of Rome

16.title: Maran-atha
16.text: Paul closes with an Aramaic word, 'Maran-atha', meaning 'Our Lord, come' (or 'The Lord comes'). It is an echo of the prayer of the first Christian communities, who eagerly awaited Christ's return.

16.question: How does Paul close the letter?
options: With a greeting in his own hand and his blessing of grace and love
With a curse on the Gentiles

pairs.right: Offering for the poor church of Jerusalem
Where Paul would stay until Pentecost
Household that was the firstfruits of Achaia
Our Lord, come
